title: Comparing Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) and Project Schedule
summary: Explains how a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) focuses on deliverables while a Project Schedule focuses on activities, highlighting their distinct purposes, contents, and how the WBS feeds into schedule creation.
content: A Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) is a hierarchical, deliverable‑oriented decomposition of the total scope of work. It lists *what* must be produced using noun‑based statements and does not contain timing, effort, or cost data. A Project Schedule, in contrast, is an activity‑oriented plan that details *how* the deliverables will be produced. It includes verb‑based task descriptions, durations, resource assignments, dependencies, milestones, and cost information. The WBS serves as the blueprint for the schedule: once the lowest‑level WBS elements can no longer be expressed as nouns, they are broken down into activities that become the schedule entries.
can_answer: How does a WBS differ from a project schedule?
Can a WBS be used as a schedule?
examples: Exhibit 1 – Mountain Bike WBS (deliverables) vs. Exhibit 2 – Project Schedule (activities)
